coredata.dc:title: Quercetin mediated gold nanoclusters explored as a dual functional nanomaterial in anticancer and bio-imaging disciplines
coredata.dc:creator: Lakshmi, Buddolla Anantha
Kim, Sanghyo
coredata.dc:description: Quercetin-mediated gold nanoclusters (Qu-GNCs) were prepared for the first time by employing bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a stabilizer with a natural flavonoid, “quercetin” as a reducing agent. Interestingly, quercetin (Qu) forms a cluster by reacting with gold under alkali conditions in the presence of BSA. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of obtained nanoclusters exhibit the optimal size with homogenous distribution. Similarly, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) confirmed the conjugation between Qu and GNCs and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra showed the bright yellowish-orange fluorescence of Qu-GNCs and they are stable over a long period of time. Further, Bio-atomic force microscopy (Bio-AFM) and confocal imaging of A549 (lung cancer cell lines) highlighted the cell morphology and cluster’s efficacy, respectively. Moreover, as-synthesized Qu-GNCs exerted high and minimal toxicities on A549 and HDFa (fibroblast cells). The obtained results are very interesting and have shown that Qu-GNCs are promising anti-cancer agents as well as potential bio-imaging nanohybrids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quercetin-mediated gold nanocluster
Flavonoids
Anticancer
Atomic force microscopy
Bio-imaging
A549 cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Live and dead cell staining assay
Measurement of the Reactive oxygen species (ROS)
Optical and PL studies of the Qu-GNCs
FT-IR and XRD studies of Qu-GNCs
pH stability studies and salt effect of GQNCs
Cell proliferation assay of Qu-GNCs in A549 and HDFa cell lines
Cell toxicity and viability studies by the live and dead assay
Evaluation of morphological changes in A549 cell lines
Application of Qu-GNCs as fluorescent material in cancer cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectra for (a) quercetin (Qu) (b) Qu-GNCs, (B) Fluorescence emission spectrum of Qu-GNCs, inset image shows the left: bright-field, right: under UV irradiation, (C) Fluorescence stability of Qu-GNCs over a period of 35 days, inset image shows corresponding fluorescence images. (D) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of Qu-GNCs (excitation wavelengths from 360 to 540 nm).
(A) FT-IR spectra of (a) quercetin (b) Qu-GNCs, (c) BSA, (B) XRD spectrum of Qu-GNCs.
(A) TEM image of Qu-GNCs, (B, C, D) HRTEM images of Qu-GNCs, (E) SAED pattern of Qu-GNCs, and (F) Bio-AFM height illustration of Qu-GNCs.
(A) PL (photoluminescence) response of Qu-GNCs at different pH values (3–10) and (B) Salt effect of Qu-GNCs (PL emission spectra at different concentrations of NaCl (1–5 mM)).
MTT assay for assessing cytotoxicity of (A) A549 cancer cell lines and (B) HDFa cell lines treated with HAuCl4, quercetin, and Qu-GNCs. Error bars representing Mean ± SD (n = 3) *p < 0.05.
Live and dead assay of A549 and HDFa cell lines at different time periods of 0, 12, and 24 h.
Morphological changes observed in A549 cell lines treated with Qu-GNCs by using Bio-AFM at different time periods of 0, 12, and 24 h.

(A) Qu-GNCs induced ROS generation in A549 cell lines (b–d; 50, 100, and 150 μg mL−1) compared with untreated cells (a). Effect of Qu-GNCs (50 μg /mL) was abrogated (e) in the presence of the N-acetyl cysteine shown declined ROS generation. (B) Fluorescence intensity of ROS generation induced by Qu-GNCs using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFDA) as a standard fluorescent probe. Error bars representing Mean ± SD (n = 3) *p < 0.05.

Laser confocal imaging of A549 cells treated with Qu-GNCs at different excitation wavelengths. (A) Bright-field, (B) blue channel (405 nm), (C) green channel (488 nm), (D) red channel (543 nm), and (E) merged.

head.ce:title: Quercetin mediated gold nanoclusters explored as a dual functional nanomaterial in anticancer and bio-imaging disciplines

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Seongnam, 461-701, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quercetin-mediated gold nanoclusters (Qu-GNCs) were prepared for the first time by employing bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a stabilizer with a natural flavonoid, “quercetin” as a reducing agent. Interestingly, quercetin (Qu) forms a cluster by reacting with gold under alkali conditions in the presence of BSA. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of obtained nanoclusters exhibit the optimal size with homogenous distribution. Similarly, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) confirmed the conjugation between Qu and GNCs and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra showed the bright yellowish-orange fluorescence of Qu-GNCs and they are stable over a long period of time. Further, Bio-atomic force microscopy (Bio-AFM) and confocal imaging of A549 (lung cancer cell lines) highlighted the cell morphology and cluster’s efficacy, respectively. Moreover, as-synthesized Qu-GNCs exerted high and minimal toxicities on A549 and HDFa (fibroblast cells). The obtained results are very interesting and have shown that Qu-GNCs are promising anti-cancer agents as well as potential bio-imaging nanohybrids.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last couple of decades, nanotechnology is exhibiting enormous impact in cancer therapy by progressing efficacy, reducing toxicity on normal cells by means of sophisticated enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect in association with active cellular uptake [1,2]. To compensate the failures of conventional drugs, nano-based materials evolved into the promising therapeutic tools owing to their nanosize, high surface to volume ratios, self-assembly capacity, biocompatibility, and encapsulation efficiency properties [3–6]. Further advancements are associated with the nanotechnology are biocompatibility and biodegradability, which provide a multifaceted attitude of nanomaterials in clinical use [7–9]. Interestingly, among the various types of metal-nanomaterials, GNCs (gold nanoclusters) are one with several applications of drug delivery, bio-sensing, bio-imaging, bio-labeling, and other vaccine delivery systems due to their optical, physicochemical, and biocompatible nature along with the potential fluorescent properties [10,11]. In many instances, flavonoids are proved as a biocompatible and potential form of therapeutics and help to improve the efficacy of nanomaterial in performing various applications. Quercetin (Qu) (3,3′,4′,5,7pentahydroxyflavone) is one of a desirable bio-flavonoids with extraordinary properties like anti-oxidation, apoptosis-inducing, cell cycle modulation, angiogenesis inhibition capacity, anti-inflammation, anticancer, antiallergy, antidiabetic nephropathic, and antiulcer [12–18]. Interestingly, Qu exerting anticancer effects by blocking the cell cycle at G1/S transition or G2/M phases and its important molecular targets include p21, p27, topoisomerase II, and cyclin B [19]. Furthermore, free radical scavenging capacity of Qu can able to reduce cancer by recognizing three reactive centers including i) o-dihydroxyl format in the B-ring, ii) 2,3-double bond infusion with the 4-oxo function in a C-ring iii) 3- and 5-hydroxyl groups along with the hydrogen bonding to the keto group [20–22]. However, combined therapy using nanomaterials have shown better treatment options comparatively than individual treatment of therapeutic agents [23]. Therefore, in the present study, we have synthesized Qu-GNCs for the first time with fascinating features like low toxicity and high biocompatibility. Furthermore, we used bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a stabilizer in the formation of Qu-GNCs due to their significant advantages in bio-imaging, bio-sensing, self-assembly, etc. [24,25]. As cancer evolved as the most common causes of death from many decades due to the i) cancer cell heterogeneity, ii) chemoresistance, and iii) improper tumor uptake [26]. Hence, we synthesized a novel Qu-GNCs, which demonstrated better cellular uptake. However, as-synthesized Qu-GNCs are emerged as a dual-functional tool for anticancer and bioimaging applications. We further focused on free radical scavenging capacity of as-synthesized Qu-GNC nanomaterials in detail. The obtained results are very interesting and have a considerable impact on future cancer research.

Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), Quercetin, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), 2, 7′-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate were purchased from Sigma -Aldrich, USA. Bovine serum albumin was purchased from Millipore LEE (South Korea). Fibroblast cells (HDFa) and their particular growth media with associated supplements were acquired from CEFO Ltd, Seoul, South Korea. Cell culture flasks were purchased from NEST Scientific, USA. Penicillin-streptomycin solutions, Trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco Laboratories, Korea. Fetal BSA was purchased from Young in Frontier, Korea. Live-dead cell staining and MTT assay kits were attained from Bio Vision Milpitas, USA. TEM sample grid was obtainedfrom Ted Pella, Inc. (USA). The Spectra/Pro® dialysis membrane (MWCO: 0.5–1 kDa) was purchased from Biotech CE Tubing, Spectrum labs, South Korea. All chemicals and other reagents were of pharmaceutical grade and used without any further purification.

UV-vis absorption spectra for the synthesized Qu-GNCs were obtained by Shimadzu UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2550). FT-IR spectra of the Qu-GNCs were measured using a JASCO FTIR-4600 spectrometer in ATR mode. The fluorescence property was recorded by a fluoro-luminescence spectrometer (Photon Technology International, Quanta Master, NJ, and USA). The morphological features of as-prepared Qu-GNCs were studied using a JPK NanoWizard II bioatomic force microscope and high-resolution FEI Titan 80–300 transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies (Scintag-SDS 2000) for the sample were obtained at 40 kV and 30 mA in the continuous-scanning 2θ mode. Laser scanning confocal microscope (Nikon eclipse TE 2000-U) was used to analyze the stained samples.

Dissolve 10 mg of quercetin in 5 mL of diluted dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution. 5 mL of 10 mM Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) solution was prepared and heated at 50 °C with magnetic stirring at 1000 rpm. To this, BSA solution was added while stirring. Meanwhile, Qu solution was added dropwise to the gold chloride and BSA mixture under subsequent heating and stirring. After 45 min, the pH of the solution was analyzed and adjusted to 9 with NaOH solution and maintained at 50 °C temperature with magnetic stirring for 4 h. The colour change can be observed from yellow to brown colour, which indicates the formation of Qu-GNCs. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 7500 rpm to remove the unreacted large-sized particles and the supernatant was separated. After that, Qu-GNCs were dialyzed against DI water using a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cut-off:0.5–1 kDa) for 24 h to remove the unreacted chemicals like quercetin in the reaction mixture. Finally, Qu-GNCs were freeze-dried and used for further analysis.

Human lung cancer cells (A549) were grown according to ATCC guidelines in DMEM medium amplified with 1% of penicillin-streptomycin solution and 10% of FBS. Simultaneously HDFa cells were cultured with its supplements by following manufacturer’s guidelines as healthy control cell lines. Both the cell lines are incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2, conditions followed by sub-culture until reaching 70–90% confluency.

Cultured A549 and HDFa cells were grown upin a 96-well plate accompanied by the incubation for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 until 60% confluency achieved. Then the medium was replaced with freshly prepared medium along with the different concentrations of treatment ranging (0, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, and 150 μg mL−1). After 24 h incubation, the medium was changed with 50 μL of the serum-less medium and 50 μL of MTT reagent. Then those are incubated for 3 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 conditions. After incubation, the medium and MTT reagent were withdrawn and 150 μL of MTT solvent was replaced. The plate was assigned on the orbital shaker for 10 min and covered with foil. The absorbance was checked and measured at 570 nm by using a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live and dead cell staining assay
ce:section.ce:para: Live and dead cell staining assay was performed according to the manufacturer's instructions and then processed by using NIKON live cell capture software and the images were obtained. Live cells were stained with green colour and dead cells were stained with red colour by the cell-permeable fluorescent dye and non-permeable fluorescent dye respectively.

Cells were carefully cultivated at a density of 5 × 105 cells /well on pre-treated coverslips with 0.2% gelatine in a six-well plate. The cells were allowed to incubate for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2 conditions and then the cells were treated with Qu-GNCs for about 0, 12, and 24 h time intervals. After incubation, the cells were cleansed with PBS twice and 4% paraformaldehyde solution was used as fixation reagent, for about 30 min. The cells were again cleansed twice with PBS, dried at room temperature and finally, image processing was done by using AFM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the Reactive oxygen species (ROS)
ce:section.ce:para: ROS generation after the addition of Qu-GNCs was assessed as per the manufacturer's instructions (Cell Biolabs, OxiselectTM Intracellular ROS assay kit). The amount of ROS was determined by quantifying the fluorescence intensity which was record using the BioTek, FLx800 fluorescent microplate reader.

Cells were cultured and grown in a six-well plate with coverslips supplemented with DMEM medium which constitute 10% of the FBS and 1% of antibiotics and undergone for 12 h incubation at 37 °C with 5% CO2. After 12 h Qu-GNCswere added up to each well and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Upon incubation, the medium was discarded and the coverslips were cleansed with PBS and maintained in the dark. Then the images were acquired by using confocal microscopy.
All the results in this paper were stated as the mean of three individualistic experiments (± standard deviation, SD) and the statistical significance was evaluated by employing the student’s t-test

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and PL studies of the Qu-GNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectroscopy studies were investigated for the as-prepared Qu-GNCs. A minute peak was observed at 280 nm, which confirmed the perfect conjugation of BSA with Au (gold) ions, as shown in Fig. 1 A (curve b) [27]. Generally, conduction electrons present on the surface of gold atoms were well-enough to react with light and produces Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak in gold nanoparticles, but in gold nanoclusters, due to their small and quantum size, the SPR peak was not observed at 520 nm wavelength region. However, the peaks observed at 260 and 380 nm in Qu (curve a) has not identified after the formation of Qu-GNCs, which further confirms the complete formation of Qu-GNCs as illustrated in Fig. 1A. Further, Fig. 1B has shown the emission peak for Qu-GNCs at a wavelength of 568 nm with orange color and inset attributes for bright-field and UV illumination images. Interestingly, as-prepared Qu-GNCs have exhibit high stability over a period of one month and the inset (Fig. 1C) represent corresponding fluorescence images. Fig. 1D shows the excitation-dependent emission properties of Qu-GNCs by tuning the excitation wavelengths from 360 to 540 nm. This behavior is beneficial, particularly in bio-imaging applications. This excitation-dependent emission behavior is dependent on the surface defects and sizes of nanoclusters [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and XRD studies of Qu-GNCs
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR studies were performed for qualitative determination and understanding regarding the formation of Qu and GNCs. The IR peaks at 1506 and 1606 cm−1 are assigned to the characteristic CC stretching frequencies of Qu (Fig. 2 A (a)). The peak at 1091 cm−1 was responsible for the characteristic benzopyran group of Qu. Interestingly, IR peak at1664 cm−1 relating to the CO stretching vibration was shifted to the wavenumber 1641 cm−1 in the Qu-GNCs with declined intensity. This demonstrates the plausible formation of Qu-GNCs by means of dative bonding between Au and CO. Furthermore, a remarkable shifting of the peak from 3274 cm−1 in Qu to 3340 cm-1 in GNCs was observed, which indicates the bonding between Au and phenolic OH of Qu. Furthermore, cyclobenzene skeleton peaks were also observed at 1521 and 1641 cm-1 in Qu-GNCs. Therefore, FT-IRstudy clearly demonstrated that the Qu was effectively formed with GNCs (Fig. 2A (b)) [29,30]. The typical stretching and bending vibrations of amide I, amide II, and amide III were noticed in the pure BSA protein at 1524, 1389, and 1240 cm−1 respectively, in as shown in curve (c). These peaks were attributed to the peptide bonds in BSA. Noteworthy, the decrease in the intensity of curve (b) was noticed compared to the curve (c), which is responsible for the conformational changes occurred in the nanoclusters. The obtained results were consistent with the reported literature [31]. Further, the powder XRD pattern of the as-produced Qu-GNCs was analyzed from 20–80°, which coincides with face-centered cubic (fcc) crystalline nature. The characteristic 2θ peaks were observed for Qu-GNCs at 38.2°, 44.37°, 64.62°, and 77.68° corresponding to (111), (200), (220), and (311) lattice planes respectively (Fig. 2B). Comparatively peak at (111) lattice plane is having high intensity than other three planes (200), (220) and (311). Noteworthy, additional three peaks were noticed at 31.7°, 45.46° and56.56° represented in star symbols (*) as unidentified peaks. However, we assumed those are formed due to the strong conjugation of quercetin with gold nanoclusters and the same was stated in the previous reports [32].

The size and height of Qu-GNCs were determined using TEM (transmission electron microscopy) and the AFM respectively. TEM image was illustrated in Fig. 3 A, with an average size of Qu-GNCs was found to be 1–3 nm with homogeneous distribution. Fig. 3B, C shows the HRTEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) results demonstrated the face-centered cubic (fcc) nature of gold (Au) with d-spacing of 0.23 nm, which corresponds to (111) plane of Au [33]. Fig. 3D illustrates the particle size distribution of as-synthesized Qu-GNCs, which comfort with the average size from 1 to 3 nm. Selected area of diffraction (SAED) pattern of Qu-GNCsindicated the typical face-centered cubic (fcc) crystalline nature of gold nanoclusters with the formation of well-dispersed rings showing (111), (220), and (311) lattice planes as shown in Fig. 3E. Further, AFM images have confirmed the height of Qu-GNCs as 5.29 nm with uniform size (Fig. 3F). SEM and elemental mapping images illustrate the presence of different elements present in Qu-GNCs as represented in Figs. S1 and S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH stability studies and salt effect of GQNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A illustrates the PL intensity of Qu-GNCs where a slight increase and decrease in pH was noticed in both acidic and basic pH media, respectively. However, these pH behaviors of Qu-GNCs were attributed to the phenomenon of protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups lying on the Qu-GNCs [34,35]. Noteworthy, at neutral pH 7, the Qu-GNCs have shown maximum PL intensity, which can be well-suitable in the bio-imaging applications. Further, in the case of Qu-GNCs, there was a uniform PL intensity feature was observed with the ionic strength of 1–5 mM NaCl. The obtained results confirm that Qu-GNCs are enough stable in ionic strength medium as illustrated in Fig. 4B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell proliferation assay of Qu-GNCs in A549 and HDFa cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: A549 and HDFa cells were treated with Qu-GNCs at different concentrations for the evaluation of cytotoxic effects employing MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] assay. The Qu-GNCs have shown better cytotoxic effects compared to HAuCl4, quercetin-treated cells alone. The obtained results from MTT assay has shown the IC50 value at 100 μg mL−1 concentration of Qu-GNCs in A549 cells, where 50% of the cell proliferation was inhibited as shown in Fig. 5 A. In the same manner, Fig. 5B illustrates the cytotoxicity of Qu-GNCs against healthy fibroblast cells (HDFa) has been evaluated which does not show significant cytotoxicity effect. Thus, it suggests the Qu-GNCs have potential cytotoxicity against A549 cell lines compared to HDFa cell lines. As-synthesized Qu-GNCs significantly inhibited the cell proliferation of A549 cell lines in a dose-dependent manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell toxicity and viability studies by the live and dead assay
ce:section.ce:para: Live and dead cell staining assay was thoroughly investigated in A549 and HDFa cell lines at different intervals of time (from 0, 12, and 24 h) using Qu-GNCs (Fig. 6 ). The increased proportions of dead cells were observed in Qu-GNCs treated A549 cell lines in a time-reliant manner from 0 to 24 h. Whereas, the minimal reduction in the number of dead cells was remarked in the case of Qu-GNCs treated HDFa cell lines. This suggests that Qu-GNCs have significant cytotoxicity effect against A549 cells and minimal effect against normal HDFa cells. The percentage of cell viability achieved in A549 and HDFa cell lines, when treated Qu-GNCs were summarized in Figs. S-3 and S-4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of morphological changes in A549 cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: Bio-AFM was employed for the determination of morphological changes induced by the Qu-GNCs (Fig. 7 ). The characteristic changes on the cell surface can be processed by the high- resolution 2D and 3D images on the cell surface. The acquired images have shown the changes in cell morphology when treated with Qu-GNCs at different time intervals. Initially, there was no change noticed in the control, but after 12 h treatment, irregular cytoplasm with rupture in the cell membrane has been observed. Eventually, after 24 h treatment, complete damage in the cellular structure has been identified; thus it suggests that as-prepared Qu-GNCs shows time-dependent cytotoxicity against A549 cells.

The ROS measurement which was induced by the Qu-GNCs in the treated A549 cells can be observed using the immunofluorescent probe (DCFDA) [36]. The Qu-GNCs enhanced oxidative stress by the generation of ROS based on their concentrations from 50 μg mL−1 and even higher concentrations of 100 and 150 μg mL-1. When compared to control, A549 cells have not shown any green fluorescence (Fig. 8 A), but the treated concentrations showed the presence of green fluorescence (Fig. 8B–D) which strongly suggests the induction of ROS production. (Fig. 8E) depicts the abrogation of ROS generation owing to Qu-GNCs by the antioxidant “N-acetyl cysteine”. (Fig. 8F) illustrates the ROS generation concerning fluorescence intensity induced by Qu-GNCs using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFDA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Qu-GNCs as fluorescent material in cancer cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Confocal fluorescence microscopy has been employed to evaluate the bio-imaging efficiency of Qu-GNCs in A549 cell lines. Qu-GNCs-treated A549 cells emit multicolour fluorescence images with blue, green, and red colors upon excitation wavelengths of 405 nm, 488 nm, and 543 nm. The multicolour fluorescence property was attributed to the excitation-dependent emission behaviour of the Qu-GNCs.These results were consistent with the previously reported literature [37]. It clearly demonstrates that Qu-GNCs are well distributed in the cells and further, can be used as propitious agents in the bio-imaging of cancer cells (Fig. 9 (A–E)).

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized Qu-mediated GNCs, which facilitates a good deal of potential anticancer and bioimaging agents. Moreover, this is the first work on the designing of gold nanoclusters with the natural flavonoid, Quercetin. Due to the striking properties such as anticancer, antioxidant, eco-friendliness, and biocompatibility, quercetin played a potential role in the generation of the nanoclusters with enhanced biological properties. Noteworthy, the results obtained from MTT and live and dead assay; provide few interesting considerations on further research in the utilization of natural flavonoids along with nanotechnology. Moreover, the confocal images also provided that as-synthesized Qu-GNCs have extraordinary benefits in the discipline of bioimaging. In addition, the high potential nanomaterial as a bioimaging probe is much advantageous in the field of cancer.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Gachon University research fund of 2018 (GCU-2018-0304) and by the R&D Program for Society of the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT of Korea (2015M3A9E2031372).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2019.02.054.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
